basics.name: Zifei Dong
basics.title: AI Product / Conversational AI Research | Chinese Dialogue Evaluation, Companion Experience, Multi-Model Strategy
summaries: I am seeking 2026 roles in AI product, conversational AI, AI companionship, model evaluation, and user-facing LLM application research.

My recent work focuses on building evaluation cases, product hypotheses, and structured evaluation workflows from long-horizon real Chinese conversations, with an emphasis on implicit need recognition, relational continuity, and companion-style interaction.

I am especially interested in the factors that determine whether users keep talking to AI over time: response stance, pacing, search/tool use, closure style, and whether a model can switch tone appropriately across contexts.
basics.metaDescription: Zifei Dong focuses on Chinese dialogue evaluation, companion assistant strategy, multi-model behavior research, and user-facing AI product work.

paragraphs: My research focuses on AI systems in Chinese contexts that users return to repeatedly. I am especially interested in implicit need recognition, relational continuity across multi-turn interactions, and supportive experiences in companion-oriented scenarios, rather than only whether a single response is correct.

What I offer is a more product-oriented research workflow: distilling long-term real usage into high-value cases, building rubrics and error taxonomies, and organizing cross-model behavioral comparisons into structured findings that can inform assistant design, evaluation, and strategy discussions.

Before shifting toward conversational AI and Human-AI Interaction, I worked extensively in medical imaging, complex data modeling, and domain-adaptive LLM training. That background makes me naturally approach user understanding, evaluation design, model behavior, and engineering implementation as parts of one connected system.

focus.title: What Determines Whether Users Continue Talking to AI in Long-Term Interaction
focus.intro: I focus on AI systems people return to over time, because in long-term interaction, the core experience is often shaped less by one-turn correctness and more by whether the model can keep giving responses that users want to respond to.

projectsHeader.title: Core Projects
projectsHeader.intro: My most important current research and practice: extracting problems from long-horizon real interactions, building evaluation and comparison frameworks, and turning results into evidence that supports assistant design and model strategy decisions.

projects.name: Chinese Dialogue Evaluation and Analysis Project
projects.title: Behavior Evaluation for Chinese Emotional Support and Companion-Style Interaction
projects.description: A public Chinese dialogue behavior benchmark distilled from long-horizon real conversations, designed to evaluate model performance in implicit need recognition, relational continuity, emotional support, and calibration scenarios, with additional multi-rater sensitivity checks.
features: Built a public evaluation asset with 16 core cases, 4 user-state buckets, 48 baseline outputs, and 4 multi-rater validation cases
Developed a semi-automated distillation workflow from private long conversations to patterns, case sets, rubrics, error taxonomies, and strategy hypotheses
Organized findings into a public static showcase, project report, and multi-model comparison evidence for recruiting, product discussion, and evaluation-method exchange

projects.name: Implicit Needs and Interaction Pattern Research
projects.title: Implicit Need Modeling and Interaction Pattern Research in Long-Horizon Human-AI Dialogue
projects.description: An ongoing research project on recurring implicit needs and interaction patterns in long-horizon real human-AI conversations. It examines how models identify confirmation-seeking, understanding tests, low-explicitness expression, and calibration needs, and why current assistants are better at remembering facts than adapting to the same user's emotional needs across changing contexts over time.
features: Analyzing four high-value implicit-need patterns: asking for advice while actually seeking confirmation; sounding analytical while actually testing understanding; expressing needs indirectly and requiring proactive sensing; and having a prior stance while needing calibration or challenge

Studying repeated patterns in long-horizon dialogue, such as recurring mentions of work, relationships, or life stress during specific periods, and how these reflect more stable emotional signals rather than one-off factual content

Summarizing common long-term memory biases in current models: they are better at reinforcing event-level user tags, but weaker at tracking shifting emotional needs and response preferences across time and context

experienceHeader.title: Research Directions
experienceHeader.intro: I continue to study the factors that shape whether users want to keep talking to AI in long-term interaction, including implicit need recognition, supportive response stance, trust formation, and how these can be expressed structurally in evaluation and product strategy.

experience.title: User Signal Modeling
experience.label: Interaction Quality
responsibilities: Study whether models can detect uncertainty, confirmation-seeking, subtle emotion, and deeper underlying intent when users do not state their needs directly.
Analyze how assistants can turn low-explicitness signals into more appropriate next responses.

experience.title: Dialogue Evaluation Design
experience.label: Benchmark Design
responsibilities: Design long-horizon dialogue case sets, rubrics, and error taxonomies to evaluate response stance, pacing, and relational continuity.
Turn qualitative interaction observations into reusable structured evaluation assets.

experience.title: Long-Horizon Interaction Pattern Analysis
experience.label: Longitudinal Observation
responsibilities: Analyze how implicit needs, confirmation-seeking, low-explicitness expression, and calibration needs recur across time and scenarios in long-horizon real human-AI conversations.
Compare model defaults in memory use, response stance, and style switching, and examine why models more easily remember facts than adapt to changing emotional needs.

experience.title: Companion Assistant Strategy
experience.label: Product Direction
responsibilities: Study when assistants in emotionally loaded conversations should comfort, probe further, calibrate, or give users more autonomy.
Use these patterns to inform default response strategy, switching conditions, and boundary design in companion-oriented products.

skillsHeader.title: Methods and Tools
skillsHeader.intro: These are the main methods I use to turn long-term interaction observations into cases, evaluation assets, and product judgments, along with the tools I use for analysis and prototyping.

technicalBackground.title: Relevant Technical Experience
technicalBackground.intro: Beyond dialogue evaluation and product research, I also have a strong modeling and engineering background. This helps me bring user understanding, evaluation design, model behavior, and implementation constraints together more naturally in Human-AI Interaction work.

items.title: Domain-Adaptive LLM Training
items.description: Worked on domain-adaptive LLM projects for long-context understanding, structured information extraction, and fine-grained text classification, responsible for Qwen-based training, tuning, and experiment iteration, with familiarity in QLoRA, SGLang, CoT, and RLHF-style alignment.

items.title: Medical Imaging Generation and Analysis
items.description: Independently led research in medical imaging generation and analysis, handling 8TB+ heterogeneous data and more than 50,000 CT cases, with experience in complex data construction, weakly supervised learning, and end-to-end training optimization.

items.title: High-Noise Data Modeling and Engineering Optimization
items.description: Gained quantitative and data engineering internship experience involving large-scale data cleaning, feature generation, training pipeline optimization, and key-module acceleration, with the ability to turn research prototypes into stable and reusable workflows.

education.degree: B.S. in Computer Science and Statistics
education.institution: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

contact.title: Currently Seeking 2026 Full-Time Opportunities in AI Product, Conversational AI, and Model Evaluation
contact.description: I am especially interested in AI product, conversational AI, AI companionship, model evaluation, and user-facing LLM application research. If your team works on long-term interaction, Chinese dialogue experience, assistant strategy, personalized responses, or evaluation systems, feel free to reach out.
